crime: She and her cellmate shared a cell, and that cellmate was the one bond she kept close; she had watched her long enough to know her evenings — reading in the library, then back along the gallery above the courtyard.
The cellmate's things were bundled by the cell door, to move to a cell of her own that had come open — the sign she had watched for all her life, that the one she kept was already leaving.
One evening she waited down in the courtyard while the cellmate read above.
When the cellmate came to the iron railing over the courtyard, she drew her straight toward where she stood below; the cellmate went over it, and the fall to the stone finished it.
The cellmate lay on the courtyard stone, well out from the foot of the wall, toward the fountain.
A body that topples the railing on its own normally drops at the wall's foot; this one had been carried outward as it fell.
Such a path means a pull from out in the courtyard. That evening, she was standing at the fountain.
fields.epitaph: She held her close enough to watch, and lost her all the same.
fields.epithet: She Who Would Not Be Left
execution: The execution stand rose bearing a tall post, a wheel fixed at its top.
A collar closed on her throat; from it the cord ran up over the wheel and down again, its end tied to a small cart on a straight track at her feet.
While the cart stayed beside her, the cord hung slack; then the cart was driven out along the track, away from her.
She held the cart back until her arms gave, then reached after it; the farther it ran, the higher the cord drew her.
The cart ran to the end of the track and stopped, and she hung at the post.

magic.name: The Draw
magic.text: She can draw a person she has bound to herself straight to her side from any distance, and that person cannot resist the pull.
